title: BEA NIPA Handbook Chapter 2 - Fundamental Concepts
content: BEA's NIPA Handbook Chapter 2 on Fundamental Concepts sets the production boundary for the national accounts. Economic production is defined in the international System of National Accounts as an activity carried out under the control and responsibility of an institutional unit that uses inputs of labour, capital, and goods and services to produce outputs of goods or services. In the U.S. accounts the boundary is further restricted by functional and practical considerations. Certain household activities such as housework, do-it-yourself projects, and care of family members are excluded, partly because by nature these activities tend to be self-contained and have limited impact on the rest of the economy and because their inclusion would affect the usefulness of the accounts for long-standing analytical purposes, such as business cycle analysis. Illegal activities are excluded because they are conducted out of sight of public scrutiny and so data are not available to measure them. BEA states that, in the NIPAs, GDP is defined as the market value of the goods, services, and structures produced by the economy in a given period. Conceptually, this measure can be arrived at by three separate means: as the sum of goods and services sold to final users, as the sum of income payments and other costs incurred in the production of goods and services, or as the sum of the value added at each stage of production. A distinction is made between domestic measures and national measures. Domestic measures cover activities that take place within the geographic borders of the United States, while national measures cover activities that are attributable to U.S. residents. Imputations are made for certain goods and services that have no observable price, such as the services provided by owner-occupied housing, so that GDP remains invariant to whether a house is owned or rented. These concepts are how the Bureau of Economic Analysis grounds real GDP as a consistent measure of current production across quarters and years.
